1: Job responds to Eliphaz’s second speech. The dialogic pattern of the book is at its most intense in the second cycle: the Friends attack more personally; Job’s responses become more anguished and more direct in their address to God. Chapter 16 is often considered one of the high points of the book’s poetry — the complaint against God as warrior (vv9–14) and the appeal to the heavenly witness (vv18–21) both appear here. The speech is also structured around the contrast between the Friends’ false comfort and the genuine witness Job seeks in heaven.
2: “I have heard all this before. You are miserable comforters, every one of you.” Mᵉnaḥămê ʿāmāl (comforters of trouble/toil) — the bitter reversal of the Friends’ stated purpose. They came to “comfort” (lᵉnaḥamô, Job 2:11) but have produced ʿāmāl (toil, grief, trouble). The term ʿāmāl encompasses both the labor of suffering and the grief it produces — the Friends have intensified Job’s suffering rather than relieving it. This is the paradigmatic indictment of theological counsel that prioritizes systematic correctness over genuine compassion with the sufferer.
3: “Will these empty words never stop? What gives you the confidence to keep answering me?” Diḇrê-rûaḥ (words of wind/breath) — the same accusation Eliphaz made against Job (Job 15:2: hot east wind) is turned back on the Friends. Mah-yamrîṣᵉḵā kî taʿᵃneh (what provokes/emboldens you to keep answering?) — the verb mārēṣ (to strengthen, provoke, make bold) suggests that something external is driving the Friends’ persistence. Job cannot understand why they continue when their words provide no relief. The exhaustion of unproductive theological debate is palpable.
4: “I could talk just like you if you were in my place — I could pile words against you and shake my head at you.” Job performs the perspectival reversal that the Friends have systematically refused to do. Lûle ʾᵃnî kᵉnafšᵉḵem (if I were in your place) — the empathy exercise the Friends did for seven days (Job 2:13) but abandoned when they began to speak. The head-shaking (ʾānîṭāh ʿᵃlêḵem bᵉrōʾšî) is the gesture of contemptuous pity (Psalms 22:7; Jeremiah 18:16; Lamentations 2:15) — the same gesture Job endures. He is imagining delivering to them what they are delivering to him.
5: “I would strengthen you with my words, and the comfort of my lips would ease your grief.” The counterfactual comfort Job would offer — genuine strengthening (ʾᵃḥazzēq bᵉḵem bᵉmî pî) rather than accusation. The verse is both accusatory (the Friends’ comfort fails this standard) and self-revelatory (Job understands what genuine comfort looks like). The comfort of the lips (nîḏ śᵉpāṯay yaḥśôḵ) that restrains grief is the wisdom literature’s ideal of therapeutic speech (Proverbs 15:4; Job 12:25). Job has internalized the wisdom ideal; the Friends have violated it.
6: “If I speak, the pain is not relieved; and if I stay silent, how much of it leaves me?” The double bind of unrelievable suffering: speech brings no relief (lōʾ-yeḥaś kᵉʾēḇî), but silence relieves none of it either (mah-minnî yaʿᵃḇōr). This existential paralysis — no action available that would improve the situation — is what distinguishes Job’s suffering from ordinary trial. It is also the theological problem at the center of the book: if there is no action Job can take that would change his situation, then either his suffering is beyond human remedy (which it is) or God must act directly (which Job demands).
7: “But now he has utterly exhausted me; he has made desolate everyone around me.” Job shifts from the Friends to God as the source of his desolation. ʿAttāh hilʾāh (now he has exhausted/made weary) — lāʾāh means to be worn out, exhausted to the point of breakdown. The desolation of Job’s community (kol-ʿāḏāṯî) — everyone around him is also affected. Job’s suffering is not merely individual; it has shattered his social world. Compare Job 19:13–19 where Job catalogs the social alienation in detail. The corporate dimension of the suffering is part of what makes it appear like divine curse rather than mere tragedy.
8: “You have wasted my flesh until it shows — that itself is a witness; my emaciation rises up and testifies against me to my face.” Job’s body has become evidence — but evidence of the opposite of what the Friends claim. They argue his body-as-ruin proves his guilt; Job says his body-as-ruin is the witness to his unjust treatment. Wᵉtiqmᵉṭēnî lᵉʿēḏ (it has become a witness against me) — the physical wreckage of Job’s body testifies, but to what? The ambiguity is deliberate: the same visible evidence supports opposite verdicts depending on the interpretive framework. Job’s framework reads the suffering as testimony against God’s justice; the Friends’ framework reads it as testimony against Job’s righteousness.
9: “He has torn me apart in his wrath and pursued me; he gnashes his teeth at me; my enemy fixes his sharpened gaze upon me.” The divine-warrior attack imagery begins in earnest. ʾAppô ṭārap wayiśṭᵉmēnî (in his anger he has torn and hated/pursued me) — ṭārap is the verb used for predatory animals tearing prey (Genesis 37:33; Job 44:28; Hosea 6:1). The teeth-gnashing (yiḥᵃrōq ʿālay bᵉšinnāyw) and the enemy-gaze (ṣārî yilṭôš ʿênāyw lî) are the gestures of predator toward prey. Job has reversed the creation-order: instead of the divine Creator caring for the creature, the divine Warrior hunts it. This language of God-as-predator is the most extreme form of the book’s theodicy-challenge and has direct parallels in Lamentations 3:1–18.
10: “They open their mouths wide at me; they strike my cheek in contempt; they have gathered themselves together against me.” The human mockers join the divine assault: pāʿᵃrû ʿālay bᵉpîhem (they gape at me with their mouths) — the gesture of contemptuous mockery (Psalms 22:13; Lamentations 2:16; Job 3:46). The cheek-strike (lᵉḥît yakkûnî bᵉḥerpāh) is a gesture of profound humiliation in the ancient Near East (1 Kings 22:24; Micah 5:1; Isaiah 50:6). The convergence of divine assault (v9) and human humiliation (v10) creates the double abandonment — by God and by human community simultaneously — that will culminate in Job 19:13–22.
11: “God has surrendered me to the evildoers and thrown me into the hands of the wicked.” Yasgîrēnî ʾēl ʾel-ʿᵃwîl (God has shut me up/surrendered me to the ungodly) — sāgar (to shut up, deliver up) is the legal term for extradition or surrender to an enemy (Deuteronomy 32:30; 1 Samuel 23:11; Psalms 31:9). God is the agent who has delivered Job to his enemies rather than protecting him from them. This is the paradox of the prologue reversed from below: the reader knows God permitted Satan to afflict Job; Job experiences it as God handing him over to the wicked. Both perspectives are simultaneously true, which is part of the book’s theological depth.
12: “I was living in ease, and he shattered me; he grabbed me by the neck and broke me apart; he stood me up as his target.” Šālēw hāyîtî wayyᵉparpᵉrēnî (I was at ease and he shattered me) — šālēw (quiet, at rest, at ease) is Job’s pre-disaster condition; pārpar (to shake apart, shatter) is its violent reversal. The neck-seizure (wayyōʾḥᵃzēnî bᵉʿōrep) and being set up as target (wayyᵉśîmēnî lô lᵉmatarrāh) — matarrāh (target) appears only here and in Lamentations 3:12 (“he bent his bow and set me as a target for his arrow”). Lamentations draws on Job’s vocabulary, suggesting that the exilic community’s suffering was interpreted through the Job-template.
13: “His archers surround me; he slashes into my kidneys without mercy and spills my gall on the ground.” Rabbāyw yibbāqᵃʿû kᵉlāyōṯāy (his archers/arrows break through my kidneys) — the kidneys (kᵉlāyôṯ) in ancient Near Eastern anatomy were the seat of conscience and deepest self (Psalms 7:10; Job 16:7; Jeremiah 11:20; Job 17:10). To pierce the kidneys is to penetrate to the innermost person. Yišpōḵ lāʾāreṣ mᵉrērāṯî (he pours out my gall on the ground) — the gall bladder spilling is a clinical description of catastrophic internal injury. The combination of military imagery (archers, breaching) and anatomical detail produces an image of total devastation of the interior life.
14: “He breaks through me, breach after breach; he charges at me like a great warrior.” Yiprᵉṣēnî pereṣ ʿal-pᵉnê-pāreṣ (he breaks through me, breach upon breach) — the pāreṣ (breach) imagery from siege warfare: a wall breached multiple times cannot be repaired. Yārûṣ ʿālay kᵉgibbôr (he charges at me like a warrior) — the gibbôr (mighty man, hero) was the elite warrior of ancient armies. God assaulting Job like a siege-engineer breaching walls and a soldier charging is the culmination of the warrior imagery in vv9–14. The passage as a whole is one of the most powerful descriptions of divine assault in the OT and is echoed structurally by Lamentations 3.
15: “I have sewn sackcloth over my skin and pressed my dignity down into the dust.” Job’s outward mourning-posture matches his inward devastation. Tāpārtî śaq ʿᵃlê giltî (I sewed sackcloth over my skin) — sackcloth (śaq) was the standard garment of mourning, grief, and petition (Genesis 37:34; Joel 1:8,13; Jonah 3:6–8). Wᵉʿôlaltî bāʿāpār qarnî (and I have thrust my horn into the dust) — the horn (qeren) was the symbol of dignity, strength, and honor in the ancient world (1 Samuel 2:1,10; Psalms 75:4–5; Job 92:10; Job 148:14). Pressing the horn into the dust is the act of complete self-abasement, the reversal of honored status.
16: “My face is flushed raw from weeping, and the shadow of death rests on my eyelids.” Pānay ḥᵃmarmarāh minnî-ḇeḵî (my face is red/inflamed from weeping) — the physical marks of prolonged grief. Ṣalmāweṯ ʿal-ʿappappāy (shadow of death on my eyelids) — ṣalmāweṯ appears again, now not as the place of death but as a presence settled on Job’s face. The eyelids as the location of ṣalmāweṯ is striking: the organ of sight is covered by death-shadow. Job’s vision is clouded by proximity to death. Despite this, v17 immediately states his innocence — the external marks of death-sentence do not correspond to interior guilt.
17: “Yet no violence has been done by my hands, and my prayer has been pure.” The contrastive ʿal lōʾ-ḥāmās bᵉkappāy (yet no violence in my palms) — the palms as the locus of moral action in the ancient world. Ḥāmās (violence, wrong) is one of the strongest terms for unjust oppression in the OT (Genesis 6:11; Ezekiel 28:16; Habakkuk 1:2–3). ûṣᵉlāṯî zakāh (and my prayer has been pure/clear) — the purity of prayer reflects the purity of moral standing. This verse is the hinge between the warrior-assault passage (vv9–16) and the witness-appeal (vv18–21): the suffering described in vv9–16 is unjust precisely because of the innocence stated in v17.
18: “O earth, do not cover my blood; let my cry find no place to settle!” The appeal to the earth as witness-for-the-prosecution is grounded in the Abel tradition: Abel’s blood cried out from the ground where it was covered (Genesis 4:10–11). Uncovered blood in the ancient world was a legal problem: it demanded justice and could not rest until satisfied (Numbers 35:33; Isaiah 26:21; Ezekiel 24:7–8). By appealing for his blood to remain uncovered (ʾal-tᵉḵasî dāmî ʾāreṣ), Job is inviting the earth itself to carry his cry for justice upward until it is answered. The earth-as-witness sets up the heaven-as-witness in v19.
19: “Even now my witness is in heaven, and my advocate is on high.” ʿēḏî baššāmayim (my witness in the heavens) and śāhᵃḏî bammᵉrômîm (my testifier on high) — this is the most significant intercessor-passage in the second cycle. Who is this heavenly witness? Interpretive options: (1) God himself — a split between God the antagonist (vv9–14) and God the advocate; (2) an angelic figure in the divine council parallel to the meliṣ of Job 33:23; (3) Job’s own righteousness personified as a heavenly witness. The most natural reading in context is that Job is appealing to God against God — a desperate appeal to God’s own integrity to vindicate what God’s actions seem to deny. This will reach its climax in Job 19:25–27 (the gōʾēl).
20: “My friends scorn me, yet my eye pours out tears to God.” The social abandonment — friends who mock (mᵉlîṣay rēʿāy, my intercessors/friends are my scorners) — against the persistent upward orientation of weeping to God. ʾEl-ʾĕlôah (to God) — the tears are directional; they go not downward into the earth but upward toward God. The paradox: God is both the cause of the suffering (vv9–14) and the address of the tears (v20). This is not contradiction but the structure of biblical lament: the complaint is directed to the very One who is accused, because there is no other court of appeal.
21: “If only a mortal man could plead with God the way a man pleads for his neighbor.” The môkîaḥ-longing from 9:33 returns in different form: wᵉyôkîaḥ lᵉgeḇer ʿim-ʾĕlôah ûḇen-ʾāḏām lᵉrêʿēhû (if a man could argue with God as a man argues for his neighbor). The legal advocacy image (yākaḥ, to argue/contend in legal setting) applied to the impossible case: a human being pleading another human’s case before God. Job longs for this structure to exist between humanity and God. The NT answer is the incarnate High Priest who was in every way like his brothers (Hebrews 2:17–18; Job 4:15–16): one who can plead humanity’s case from within the human condition.
22: “For the few years left are slipping past, and I am walking a road from which there is no return.” Šᵉnôṯ mispar yᵉʾĕṯāyû (the years of number/few years will come) — Job’s awareness that time is running out for the vindication he seeks. Wᵉʾōraḥ lōʾ-ʾāšûḇ ʾehlōḵ (and I am walking a path of no return) — the one-way road to death makes the urgency of justice acute. If vindication does not come before death, it will not come at all — unless there is something beyond death (Job 19 will explore this). The speech ends with the one-way road, preparing for Job 17’s meditation on Sheol as the only destination.
